annotations.text: Did this patient have a community-acquired pneumonia? The increased frequency of community-acquired pneumonias is well recognized in patients infected with HIV.

Since  *Pneumocystis carinii* is the most common single pathogen causing pneumonia in patients with AIDS, occurring in 80 percent of such patients during the course of their illness, I have to include it in the differential diagnosis of this patient's disease
The lung is affected by one of these processes as the initial manifestation of AIDS in about 65 percent of patients infected with HIV.
Although these infections often present with atypical features, such as diffuse multilobar infiltration, the response to conventional antibiotic therapy is usually prompt.
Both transbronchial biopsy and bronchoalveolar lavage have a diagnostic sensitivity of at least 85 percent when used separately and 100 percent when used together.
The annual rate of pneumococcal pneumonia in the population with AIDS, for example, is at least five times higher than the rate in the general population.
Fever and weight loss are the most frequent presenting manifestations, occurring in about 80 percent and 50 percent of the patients, respectively.
Kaposi's sarcoma is the most frequently encountered tumor in persons with AIDS, affecting about 35 to 48 percent of them.
Hilar lymphadenopathy has been reported in 8 to 19 percent of patients with pulmonary Kaposi's sarcoma.
The prevalence of pulmonary Kaposi's sarcoma in the AIDS population is estimated to be 20 percent.
Because of these atypical features, tuberculosis in a patient with AIDS may easily be confused with other opportunistic infections.
Disease due to  *Mycobacterium avium–intracellulare* (also called  *M. avium* complex) is known to be prevalent among patients with AID
I am finally led to consider infection with  *Histoplasma capsulatum* in this patient

Although histoplasmosis is often disseminated at the time of presentation and symptoms can be severe, mimicking a gram-negative septicemia, subacute illness with a high fever, substantial weight loss, and minor respiratory tract symptoms is not uncommon.
Anemia is the most common hematologic disturbance in cases of disseminated histoplasmosis.
The patient was treated with intravenously administered amphotericin and responded well, with defervescence within two days, from a temperature as high as 40°C.
